slides.title: Why loops exist
body: A loop runs the same block of code many times. Without loops, you'd write the same line 100 times to process 100 records.
Real example: Spotify processes ~100 million song-plays per day. Every analytics pipeline that touches that data is built on loops — one block of logic, run once per record.

vocabulary.def: A control structure that runs a block of code repeatedly until a condition stops it.

slides.title: Apex's three loop shapes
body: 1) Counter for-loop — when you know how many times to repeat. 2) For-each loop — when you have a collection. 3) While loop — when you don't know how many times.
Plus a Salesforce-special: the SOQL for-loop, which streams query results without loading them all into memory at once.

slides.title: The counter for-loop
body: Shape: for (Integer i = 0; i < N; i++) { ... }. Three parts inside the parens: initialize, condition, advance.
Real example: a retry loop that attempts an API call up to 5 times before giving up. The counter is the attempt number.

slides.title: Counting with for
slides.intro: Type this. The classic counter loop runs 5 times.
lineExplanations: Initialize i to 0. Continue while i < 5. After each iteration, run i++ (increment by 1). The { opens the loop body.
Body runs once per iteration. Prints 'Attempt 0', 'Attempt 1', ..., 'Attempt 4'. Closing } returns to the for-line for the next iteration.

slides.title: The for-each loop
body: Shape: for (Type item : collection) { ... }. Reads naturally: 'for each item in collection, do X.'
Real example: every Slack notification batch, every email-blast Apex job, every Salesforce trigger walks a List of records this way.

vocabulary.def: Loop that walks every item in a collection without manual indexing.

slides.title: Walking a list
slides.intro: Type this. A clean for-each over a list of names.
lineExplanations: Inline list literal — three names ready to iterate.
for-each: 'name' is a fresh variable that holds one list item per iteration.
Body runs three times: 'Hello Alex', 'Hello Sam', 'Hello Jordan'.

slides.title: The SOQL for-loop
body: Apex-only superpower: for (Account a : [SELECT ... FROM Account]) { ... }. Streams records from the database in batches of 200, never loading all into memory.
Real example: a nightly batch processing 50,000 accounts. With a regular query that's 50,000 records on the heap (governor-limit failure). With a SOQL for-loop, it's 200 at a time — safe.

slides.title: Streaming SOQL
slides.intro: Type this. The brackets [...] are inline SOQL — Apex's database query syntax.
lineExplanations: Inline SOQL inside the for-each. Apex fetches up to 200 Account records and streams them to the loop variable 'a'.
Read the Name field off each Account. No List variable was ever created — the loop is the consumer.

slides.title: The while-loop
body: Shape: while (condition) { ... }. Repeats as long as the condition is true.
Real example: retry-with-backoff logic — keep retrying until the API succeeds OR you've hit the max attempts. Uber's payment service uses this pattern when calling external authorization.

vocabulary.def: Loop that runs as long as a condition stays true. Check happens BEFORE each iteration.

slides.title: While with a safety break
slides.intro: Type this. Always include a safety break in while-loops to avoid infinite loops.
lineExplanations: Counter starts at 0.
while (attempts < 5) — condition checked before each iteration. False at 5, loop exits.
Body runs while condition is true.
++ increments — without this we'd loop forever.

slides.title: break and continue
body: 'break' immediately exits the loop. 'continue' skips to the next iteration.

Real example: searching a list for the first match — break out of the loop the moment you find it instead of walking the rest. continue is for skipping invalid records (null, wrong type) without exiting.

vocabulary.def: Statement that immediately exits the enclosing loop.
Statement that skips to the next iteration of the enclosing loop.

slides.title: Skip and stop
slides.intro: Type this. continue skips negatives; break stops at 100.
lineExplanations: List with a negative, two valid values, and a 100.
Loop over each.
n=-1 hits this; continue jumps to next iteration. n=5 falls through.
n=100 hits this; break exits the loop entirely.
Only prints '5'. The 3 never gets processed because we broke at 100.

slides.front: What's the difference between break and continue?
slides.back: break exits the loop entirely. continue skips to the next iteration but the loop keeps running.
slides.detail: Use break when you've found what you need (or hit a fatal error). Use continue when you want to skip invalid items and keep processing.

slides.question: You need to print every Contact in a 50,000-record table. Which is safest in Apex?
options: List<Contact> all = [SELECT Id FROM Contact]; for (Contact c : all) { ... }
Loop the Contact list directly without querying.
slides.explanation: The SOQL for-loop streams in batches of 200. Loading 50,000 records into a List variable would explode the heap and trigger governor-limit failure.

slides.title: The cardinal sin: SOQL inside a loop
body: NEVER put a SOQL query inside a for-each loop body. Each iteration runs another query — 200 records means 200 queries, double the governor limit (100/transaction).
Real example: the #1 production-breaking pattern in junior Apex code. Bulkification is the cure (we'll go deep in the Triggers and Governor Limits lessons).

slides.title: Capstone: Retry with attempts cap
slides.intro: An external integration occasionally returns a transient error. Build the universal retry pattern: keep trying up to N attempts, break out the moment one succeeds, log the outcome either way. This shape shows up in every async job that talks to a flaky service.
lineExplanations: Cap the number of attempts.
Counter that increments on each pass.
Outcome flag — flips to true if any attempt succeeds.
while-loop runs until the counter hits the cap.
Increment first thing — guarantees the counter advances every pass (no accidental infinite loop).
Simulate a flaky operation: ~30% success rate.
If this attempt succeeded, handle and exit.
Set the success flag so the post-loop check knows.
Log which attempt won.
break exits the while-loop entirely. No more attempts.
Close the success branch.
Otherwise log the failure and the loop continues.
Close the loop body.
After the loop, if we never succeeded...
...log that we exhausted all retries.
Close the failure branch.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Three loop shapes (counter, for-each, while), the SOQL for-loop superpower, and break/continue for control flow.
Next: Methods & Modifiers — how Apex code is organized into reusable, callable units.
